title: Corner Workbench with Bench-Tool Lazy Susan
summary: An L-shaped garage workbench that solves the corner problem with a lazy susan — bench tools mount on a rotating round top and spin to face you, locked by a pull pin.
description:
Every L-shaped workbench has the same dead zone: the corner, too deep to reach and too awkward to use. This design turns that corner into the best seat in the shop — a round tabletop on lazy susan hardware, with the miter saw, grinder, or vise bolted around its edge. Spin the wheel, the tool you need faces you, drop the locking pin, work. The corner stops being wasted and becomes the tool station.

The bench itself is wall-anchored 2x4 shelf frames — two long, two short, screwed to studs at 15-1/2" and 35-1/2" heights — with just THREE legs total, because the wall carries everything else. That's why the stud attachment isn't optional here: the wall IS half the structure. Both frames at each height means a full lower shelf under the whole L, which a garage fills before the paint dries.

The locking pin is the detail that makes the lazy susan a tool station instead of a party trick: a 6" eye-bolt dropped through matched holes in the rotating top and the bench top. A running miter saw on an unlocked turntable is a genuinely bad idea — drill several lock positions (one per mounted tool) and use the pin every time.

Mount tools around the disc's rim with their working edges overhanging slightly, and balance the layout — three tools at thirds beats two tools opposite. Buy the round top (a 16–24" table round) or bandsaw one from 3/4" ply scrap.

tools.note: Framing screws, stud attachment, and the lock-pin holes.
1" brads fasten the plywood tops to the frames.
The wall lines at 15-1/2" and 35-1/2" — the bench is only as flat as these lines.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Shelf frames and the three legs.

materials.name: Lazy susan hardware kit
materials.note: A big, rated one — it's carrying cast-iron bench tools.

materials.name: Eye bolt, 6"
materials.note: The locking pin. Non-negotiable with a spinning miter saw.

cutList.note: Only three — the wall is the fourth through tenth legs.

steps.title: Build the four shelf frames
steps.body:
Frame the two long shelves (84" rails, supports on the ends plus two spaced between) and two short shelves (60" rails, one middle support) with glue and 2-3/4" screws.

Ladder frames, all four — the repetition is the build. Square each by diagonals.

steps.title: Hang the long frames on the wall
steps.body:
Find and mark the studs, then strike LEVEL lines at 15-1/2" and 35-1/2" off the floor. Screw the long frames to the studs with their top edges on those lines, then add one leg at the long shelf's outside corner.

The wall is doing the work of seven legs here, which is why every frame-to-stud screw matters and drywall gets no vote.

steps.title: Add the short frames and remaining legs
steps.body: Screw the short frames to the adjacent wall AND to the long frames where they meet in the corner. Add the inside-corner leg where the two shelves join, and the last leg at the short shelf's open end. The L is now standing on three legs and a wall, exactly as designed.

steps.title: Top it
steps.body: Glue the frame tops and lay on the 1/2" plywood — 1" brads into every rail and support. The glue matters more than the brads: glued skins turn each ladder frame into a torsion box, and a garage bench earns every bit of that stiffness.

steps.title: Lazy susan, lock pin, tools
steps.body:
Mount the round top in the corner on the lazy susan hardware. Then the safety feature: drill a hole through the turntable AND matching holes through the bench top at each position you want a tool to lock facing you — the 6" eye bolt drops through as the pin.

Bolt the bench tools around the disc's rim, balanced (thirds, not halves), working edges just proud of the edge. Spin, pin, work. USE the pin every time — a miter saw on a free-spinning turntable is a story you don't want to tell the ER intake nurse.

images.alt: L-shaped corner garage workbench with rotating bench-tool lazy susan
